title: The last eel of the canals
summary: Amsterdam's last licensed canal eel fisherman is sixty-four — and his license will not be given to anyone else.
body:
Mr Pieter has fished eels in Amsterdam for forty years. He goes out at four in the morning. His small boat moves slowly along the canals.

There are seven eel licenses in the city. Pieter has one. The other six belong to old men too. When they stop, the licenses will end. The city will not give new ones.

The water in the canals is cleaner now than thirty years ago. The eels are coming back. But the rules are old. The city says fishing is for the past. The city wants tourists, not nets.

Pieter understands. He does not fight. He just goes out at four.

'The water is quiet at four,' he says. 'The trams are not running. The boats are not running. I can hear the eels move. I am the only person who hears them.'

A young biologist visits him sometimes. She studies the eels. She wants to count them. She asks Pieter where the eels go in winter.

Pieter smiles. 'I will tell you,' he says. 'You write it in your book. After me, no fisherman will know. Maybe somebody should know.'

They sit in the boat for two hours. Pieter shows her each canal. He tells her about each one. She writes everything in a small notebook.

Later, the biologist says, 'Why did you tell me? Some fishermen keep their secrets.'

Pieter looks at the water. 'A secret only one man knows,' he says, 'is just a small thing waiting to be lost.'

annotations.meaningVi: đánh bắt cá (động từ ở quá khứ)
giấy phép (chính thức cho phép làm gì)
nghiên cứu (động từ, ngôi thứ ba số ít)
đang chờ bị mất đi (cụm bị động dạng đơn giản)

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives with 'cleaner than'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi so sánh hai thứ ở A1, dùng 'tính từ ngắn + -er than'. Trong bài: 'The water in the canals is cleaner now than thirty years ago.' Với tính từ một âm tiết, thêm -er: clean → cleaner, old → older, fast → faster. Sau 'than' là cái thứ hai được so sánh.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To compare two things at A1, use 'short adjective + -er than'. From the passage: 'The water in the canals is cleaner now than thirty years ago.' One-syllable adjectives just add -er: clean → cleaner, old → older, fast → faster. After 'than' comes the second thing being compared.

translationVi:
Ông Pieter đã bắt lươn ở Amsterdam suốt bốn mươi năm. Ông ra sông từ bốn giờ sáng. Chiếc thuyền nhỏ của ông trôi chậm dọc theo các kênh.

Thành phố có bảy giấy phép bắt lươn. Pieter có một. Sáu cái còn lại thuộc về những ông già khác. Khi họ dừng, các giấy phép sẽ chấm dứt. Thành phố sẽ không cấp giấy phép mới.

Nước trong kênh bây giờ sạch hơn ba mươi năm trước. Lươn đang trở lại. Nhưng luật cũ. Thành phố nói nghề cá là chuyện của quá khứ. Thành phố muốn du khách, không muốn lưới.

Pieter hiểu. Ông không cãi. Ông chỉ đi ra lúc bốn giờ.

'Nước yên lúc bốn giờ,' ông nói. 'Xe điện chưa chạy. Thuyền cũng chưa chạy. Tôi nghe được lươn di chuyển. Tôi là người duy nhất nghe được.'

Một nhà sinh học trẻ thỉnh thoảng đến thăm ông. Cô nghiên cứu lươn. Cô muốn đếm chúng. Cô hỏi Pieter mùa đông lươn đi đâu.

Pieter mỉm cười. 'Tôi sẽ nói cho cô,' ông nói. 'Cô viết vào sách. Sau tôi, sẽ không ngư dân nào còn biết. Có lẽ ai đó phải biết.'

Họ ngồi trên thuyền hai giờ. Pieter chỉ cho cô từng con kênh. Ông kể cho cô về mỗi kênh. Cô ghi tất cả vào một cuốn sổ nhỏ.

Sau đó, nhà sinh học nói: 'Sao ông kể cho tôi? Có ngư dân giữ bí mật.'

Pieter nhìn xuống mặt nước. 'Một bí mật chỉ một người biết,' ông nói, 'chỉ là một thứ nhỏ đang chờ bị mất.'

questions.prompt: How long has Pieter fished eels?
questions.explanation: 'Mr Pieter has fished eels in Amsterdam for forty years.'

questions.prompt: What time does he start work?
questions.explanation: 'He goes out at four in the morning.'

questions.prompt: How many eel licenses exist in the city?
questions.explanation: 'There are seven eel licenses in the city.'

questions.prompt: What will happen when the old fishermen stop?
options: The city will not give new licenses — the licenses will end
Young people will get the licenses
The eels will be moved to a lake
questions.explanation: 'The city will not give new ones.'

questions.prompt: What is the state of the canal water now?
questions.explanation: 'The water in the canals is cleaner now than thirty years ago.'

questions.prompt: Why does Pieter prefer four in the morning?
options: It is quiet — no trams or boats — so he can hear the eels move
It is the only legal time
The eels are visible at four
questions.explanation: 'The water is quiet at four. The trams are not running. The boats are not running. I can hear the eels move.'

questions.prompt: What is the young biologist's job?
options: She studies and counts the eels
She paints pictures of the canals
questions.explanation: 'She studies the eels. She wants to count them.'

questions.prompt: Why does Pieter share his knowledge with her?
options: After him, no fisherman will know — somebody should
She paid him a lot of money
questions.explanation: 'After me, no fisherman will know. Maybe somebody should know.'

questions.prompt: What does Pieter mean by 'a secret only one man knows is just a small thing waiting to be lost'?
options: Knowledge kept by only one person disappears with that person — sharing is what makes it survive
A craft dies not only when its last worker stops, but also when its knowledge is never passed on
Old men should not work alone
questions.explanation: The passage circles around the loss of knowledge.
questions.hint: Think about the title and the closing line.

questions.explanation: Six licenses belong to old men = are theirs.

Pieter smiles. 'I will tell you,' he says. 'You write it in your book. After me, no fisherman will know. Maybe somebody should {{b8}}.'

blanks.explanation: Cleaner now than thirty years ago.
He can hear the eels move.
She writes in a small notebook.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Pieter says a secret only one person knows is 'a small thing waiting to be lost.' Think of one piece of knowledge that lives only in your family — a recipe, a route, a way of doing something. Who could you teach it to in the next month, and what would be the cost of not teaching them?
